1: shadow Save me, O God! For the waters have come up to my neck. The waters of chaos threatening life — the depth-imagery that Paul applies to Christ's descent: who will descend into the abyss? (that is, to bring Christ up from the dead) (Romans 10:7). Christ takes the drowning that the sinner deserves; the waters of judgment rise to his neck so they need not overwhelm those in him.
2: shadow I sink in deep mire, where there is no foothold; I have come into deep waters, and the flood sweeps over me. The deep mire and flood sweeping over — the desolation of the cross where Christ is immersed in judgment. He who walked on water (Matthew 14:25) sinks into the mire of human sin so that those who sink might be raised with him. The flood that swept over him is the one Noah's flood typified (1 Peter 3:20-21).
3: shadow I am weary with my crying out; my throat is parched. My eyes grow dim with waiting for my God. The exhaustion of the cross: hours of crying out, throat parched — I thirst (John 19:28). Eyes growing dim waiting for God — the darkened hours of Golgotha (Matthew 27:45-46) when God's presence seems withdrawn. Christ drinks the full cup of human abandonment and waits for the Father.
4: direct More in number than the hairs of my head are those who hate me without cause; mighty are those who would destroy me, those who attack me with lies. What I did not steal must I now restore? John 15:25 cites the without cause motif from this psalm (and Psalms 35:19): they hated me without a cause — Jesus applies it directly to himself. The causeless hatred, the lying accusers, the demand that he restore what he did not take: all fulfilled in the trial and crucifixion.
5: shadow O God, you know my folly; the wrongs I have done are not hidden from you. A puzzling verse: Christ who had no folly or wrong. He speaks here as the one who has taken the position of the sinner — he made him to be sin who knew no sin (2 Corinthians 5:21). Standing in the sinner's place before God, he voices what belongs to us; God's knowledge of the wrong is answered by Christ's bearing of it.
6: shadow Let not those who hope in you be put to shame through me, O Lord GOD of hosts; let not those who seek you be dishonored through me, O God of Israel. Christ's intercession for his own: he bears shame so that they are not put to shame. Everyone who believes in him will not be put to shame (Romans 10:11, citing Isaiah 28:16). His prayer that those who hope in God not be dishonored through his suffering is answered by the resurrection.
7: shadow For it is for your sake that I have borne reproach, that dishonor has covered my face. Christ bore reproach for the Father's sake — Paul grounds this in v9b (Romans 15:3): Christ did not please himself but bore the reproach that fell on him for God's honor. The dishonor covering his face is the blindfolding and striking of the soldiers (Luke 22:64), the shame of the cross.
8: shadow I have become a stranger to my brothers, an alien to my mother's sons. Christ's alienation from family: not even his brothers believed in him (John 7:5). He became a stranger to his own (John 1:11 — he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him). The estrangement of the righteous sufferer from family is Christ's actual experience in his earthly ministry.
9: direct For zeal for your house has consumed me, and the reproaches of those who reproach you have fallen on me. Two direct citations: (1) John 2:17 — after the Temple cleansing, his disciples remembered that it was written, Zeal for your house will consume me. (2) Romans 15:3 — For Christ did not please himself, but as it is written, The reproaches of those who reproached you fell on me. Both halves of this verse are applied explicitly to Christ in the NT.
10: shadow When I wept and humbled my soul with fasting, it became my reproach. Christ's fasting in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2) and his weeping (John 11:35; Luke 19:41) — acts of genuine humility and grief that are mocked. His weeping at Lazarus's tomb caused some to mock his apparent inability to prevent death (John 11:37). Humility before God becomes the occasion for human reproach.
11: shadow When I made sackcloth my clothing, I became a byword to them. The byword — Christ as the crucified Messiah who was a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Greeks (1 Corinthians 1:23). The sackcloth-wearer who is mocked is the righteous sufferer; the cross becomes the byword of shame that God transforms into the power and wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24).
12: shadow I am the talk of those who sit in the gate, and the drunkards make songs about me. The public mockery — at the gate (the place of judgment) and in the streets. Those who mocked Christ at the trial (Matthew 26:67-68) and at the cross (Matthew 27:39-44) made him the subject of their taunts. The drunkards' songs become the soldiers' mockery: Hail, King of the Jews!
13: shadow But as for me, my prayer is to you, O LORD. At an acceptable time, O God, in the abundance of your steadfast love answer me in your saving faithfulness. The acceptable time — Paul cites Isaiah's in an acceptable time I have listened to you (2 Corinthians 6:2) to describe the present age of salvation: Behold, now is the acceptable time. Christ's prayer is answered at the acceptable time — the resurrection on the third day.
14: shadow Deliver me from sinking in the mire; let me be delivered from my enemies and from the deep waters. The deliverance from deep waters and mire — the resurrection is Christ's deliverance from the depths of death. God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (Acts 2:24). The enemy death and the deep waters of judgment are both overcome in the resurrection of the one who prays here.
15: shadow Let not the flood sweep over me, or the deep swallow me up, or the pit close its mouth over me. The pit closing its mouth — death sealing its claim over Christ. But the tomb cannot hold him (Acts 2:24 — it was not possible for him to be held by it). The pit that closed its mouth over him on Good Friday opened at the resurrection; the deep that swallowed him disgorged its conqueror.
16: revelation of God Answer me, O LORD, for your steadfast love is good; according to your abundant mercy, turn to me. God's steadfast love (chesed) is the motivation of the atonement: God so loved the world that he gave his only Son (John 3:16); in this is love, not that we have loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins (1 John 4:10). The abundant mercy that turns toward the afflicted one is made flesh in Christ.
17: shadow Hide not your face from your servant, for I am in distress; make haste to answer me. The hidden face at the cross — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matthew 27:46). The servant in distress is Christ who experiences the full weight of divine hiddenness so that his people need never experience it permanently. The resurrection is the face of God turned again toward the servant.
18: shadow Draw near to my soul, and redeem it; ransom me because of my enemies! God draws near to redeem by sending the Son: when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son... to redeem those who were under the law (Galatians 4:4-5). The ransom paid is the blood of Christ — the Son of Man came... to give his life as a ransom for many (Matthew 20:28).
19: shadow You know my reproach, and my shame and my dishonor; my foes are all known to you. God knows the reproach Christ bore — the shame of the cross, which Christ despised but endured (Hebrews 12:2). The Father's knowledge of his Son's dishonor is the ground of vindication: precisely because God knows, he acts in the resurrection to reverse every shame and honor the one who was dishonored.
20: shadow Reproaches have broken my heart, so that I am in despair. I looked for pity, but there was none, and for comforters, but I found none. The heart broken by reproach — perhaps literally (John 19:34 — blood and water from the pierced side suggests cardiac rupture). Comforters found none: disciples fled (Matthew 26:56), Peter denied (Matthew 26:69-75), the crowd mocked. The comfortless suffering is Christ's alone, so that he might be the Comforter through the Spirit (John 14:16).
21: direct They gave me poison for food, and for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink. The most direct fulfillment in the Passion: John 19:28-29 — Jesus, knowing that all was now finished, said (to fulfill the Scripture), I thirst. A jar full of sour wine stood there, so they put a sponge full of the sour wine on a hyssop branch and held it to his mouth. Matthew 27:34,48; Mark 15:36; Luke 23:36 all record the sour wine offered at the cross — the precise fulfillment of Psalms 69:21.
22: direct Let their table become a snare before them; make their sacrificial feasts a trap. Romans 11:9-10 quotes verses 22-23 to explain Israel's stumbling: And David says, Let their table become a snare and a trap, a stumbling block and a retribution for them. Paul applies this to those who rejected Christ — the covenant blessings becoming occasions of judgment for those who refused the Messiah.
23: direct Let their eyes be darkened, so that they cannot see, and make their loins tremble continually. Romans 11:10 continues the citation: let their eyes be darkened so that they cannot see, and bend their backs forever. Paul uses this verse (combined with v22) to explain the hardening of Israel that has opened the door for Gentile salvation — the stumbling of some for the fullness of others, until all Israel is saved (Romans 11:25-26).
24: shadow Pour out your indignation upon them, and let your burning anger overtake them. The divine wrath poured out — which Christ absorbed at the cross (Romans 3:25 — God presented him as a propitiation). The indignation that should fall on the sinner fell on the righteous one; those who reject his propitiating work face the undiluted wrath (Revelation 14:10).
25: direct May their camp be desolate; let no one dwell in their tents. Acts 1:20 applies this verse directly to Judas: For it is written in the Book of Psalms, Let his homestead become desolate, and let there be no one to dwell in it. Peter explicitly cites Psalms 69:25 as the scriptural warrant for Judas's fate and the need to appoint a replacement.
26: shadow For they persecute him whom you have struck, and they recount the pain of those you have wounded. Those who persecuted Christ when God struck him — the crowd that joined in the Passion, Pilate who handed him over, the soldiers who struck the already-condemned one. Paul's citation context (Rom 11) situates this in the hardening of those who would not recognize God's own Messiah in his suffering.
27: shadow Add to them punishment upon punishment; may they have no acquittal from you. The imprecation against those who rejected the righteous one — read through Christ, this is the solemn warning of Hebrews: how much worse punishment will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God (Hebrews 10:29). The no-acquittal clause is the unforgivable sin of final rejection.
28: shadow Let them be blotted out of the book of the living; let them not be enrolled among the righteous. The book of the living — the book of life (Revelation 3:5; Psalms 13:8; Psalms 20:15). Christ promises: the one who conquers will be clothed thus in white garments, and I will never blot his name out of the book of life (Revelation 3:5). Those who reject him are not enrolled among the righteous; those who receive him have their names written permanently in the Lamb's book of life.
29: shadow But I am afflicted and in pain; let your salvation, O God, set me on high! The afflicted and pained one set on high — the Ascension as God's answer to the lament. Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name (Philippians 2:9). The one who descended into the depths of pain is set on high above all creation.
30: shadow I will praise the name of God with a song; I will magnify him with thanksgiving. The praise that follows deliverance — Christ leading his brothers in praise (Hebrews 2:12, citing Psalms 22:22). The song of thanksgiving that emerges from lament is the Easter hymn — the church's praise grounded in the resurrection of the one who was in pain and is now set on high.
31: shadow This will please the LORD more than an ox or a bull with horns and hoofs. The sacrifice God prefers over bulls and oxen — the obedient heart of Christ: sacrifices and offerings you have not desired... behold, I have come to do your will (Hebrews 10:5-7, citing Psalms 40:6-8). Christ's willing self-offering pleases the Father more than all the animal sacrifices of the Levitical system combined.
32: shadow When the humble see it they will be glad; you who seek God, let your hearts revive. The humble who see and are glad — those who receive the resurrection news: but to each one of us grace was given (Ephesians 4:7). The hearts that revive are the hearts of the disciples who see the risen Lord (John 20:20 — the disciples were glad when they saw the Lord).
33: revelation of God For the LORD hears the needy and does not despise his own people who are prisoners. The God who does not despise prisoners is revealed in Christ who identifies with the prisoner: I was in prison and you came to me (Matthew 25:36); the Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to proclaim liberty to the captives (Luke 4:18). He who is God does not despise his own people who are captive.
34: shadow Let heaven and earth praise him, the seas and everything that moves in them. The universal praise directed at God through Christ — Revelation's vision: every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, and all that is in them, saying, To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever! (Revelation 5:13).
35: shadow For God will save Zion and build up the cities of Judah, and people shall dwell there and possess it; The new Jerusalem built up by God through Christ — I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God (Revelation 21:2). God saves Zion not through military might but through the Lamb — the one who was slain becomes the lamp of the new city (Revelation 21:23).
36: shadow the offspring of his servants shall inherit it, and those who love his name shall dwell in it. The inheritance of the servants' offspring — the heirs of God and co-heirs with Christ (Romans 8:17). Those who love his name shall dwell in it: in my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you (John 14:2). The dwelling is prepared by the one whose name is loved — Christ, who secures the inheritance for the offspring of his servants.
